front: Work: in this tetralogy, Honda recognizes successive reincarnations of Kiyoaki Matsugae by a distinctive pattern of three moles
Work: in this tetralogy's first novel, Kiyoaki has an affair with Satoko Ayakura and two Siamese princes search for a lost emerald ring at the Peers School
back: Spring Snow / The Sea of Fertility (Yukio Mishima)

front: Work: in the third novel of this tetralogy, Honda visits Thailand and holds a pool party at his villa to check if Ying Chan has three moles
back: The Temple of Dawn / The Sea of Fertility (Yukio Mishima)

front: Work: this tetralogy ends at Gesshu Temple, where an abbess doesn't recall another character; Honda ponders 'perhaps there has been no I' in a garden with 'no memories, nothing'
Work: in this novel, Mizoguchi burns down the title structure (based on a real-life event); he calls the temple 'a symbol of the real world's evanescence'
back: The Temple of the Golden Pavilion (Yukio Mishima)
work: The Temple of the Golden Pavilion

front: Work: in this novel, the protagonist is struck by impotence after visions of a temple and remarks 'the first real problem I faced in my life was that of beauty'
back: The Temple of the Golden Pavilion (Yukio Mishima)
work: The Temple of the Golden Pavilion

front: Work: in this novel, Kashiwagi slaps his girlfriend after asking the protagonist Mizoguchi to steal irises and cattails for ikebana
back: The Temple of the Golden Pavilion (Yukio Mishima)
work: The Temple of the Golden Pavilion

front: Work: in this semi-autobiographical 'I-novel,' Kochan explores his repressed homosexuality and has a sexual awakening while looking at Guido Reni's painting of Saint Sebastian
Work: in this novel, Kochan daydreams about cannibalizing a fellow student and pretends to be attracted to Sonoko
Work: in this novel, the narrator muses about excrement and the Earth when he sees a 'night-soil man' pass in the street
Work: in this novel, Noboru spies through a peephole on his mother Fusako having sex with the sailor Ryuji
back: The Sailor Who Fell from Grace with the Sea (Yukio Mishima)
work: The Sailor Who Fell from Grace with the Sea

front: Work: this novel ends with a gang of boys drugging a sailor's tea to dissect him
back: The Sailor Who Fell from Grace with the Sea (Yukio Mishima)
work: The Sailor Who Fell from Grace with the Sea

front: Work: in this short story, Lieutenant Shinji and wife Reiko have sex under a scroll reading 'Sincerity' before killing themselves; thought to foreshadow its author's death
Author: this author's death poem reads 'falling is the essence of a flower' (in tanka form)
Author: this author's kaishakunin (fellow Shield Society member) botched three attempts to behead him during his 1970 seppuku
Work: in this essay, words are compared to 'white ants' for their 'corrosive function'; the epilogue describes flying in an F-104 and comparing a cloud to an Ouroboros
Work: in this Mishima novel, pearl diver Shinji falls in love with the wealthy Hatsue; two young men are tested on a shipping vessel
Work: in this Mishima novel, Shunsuke crafts his protege/lover Yuichi into a weapon of revenge against women
Author: Paul Schrader directed a biopic of this author subtitled 'A Life in Four Chapters,' culminating with 'Harmony of Pen and Sword'
Author: Philip Glass's Third String Quartet is nicknamed for this author; its final chord is a pulsating E-flat major arpeggio in first inversion
Author: this author modeled for the erotic photography collection Ordeal by Roses (Punishment by Roses), posing as Saint Sebastian with arrows
Author: Henry Miller wrote Reflections on the Death of this author, imagining meeting him in Devachan
Author: South Korean author Shin Kyung-sook plagiarized from this author's story 'Patriotism'
